title: Ophthalmology - Annual Diabetic Eye Examination
author: Dr. Karen Liu

sections.reasonForVisit: Annual dilated diabetic eye examination. Type 2 diabetes mellitus x 15 years with known mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy.
sections.historyOfPresentIllness:
Mr. Morrison is a 72-year-old gentleman with type 2 diabetes mellitus for 15 years presenting for his annual diabetic eye examination. He was last seen in our office in December 2023, at which time he was noted to have bilateral mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) with scattered microaneurysms. No macular edema was present at that time.

The patient denies any new visual complaints. He reports stable vision bilaterally. No flashing lights, new floaters, curtain-like visual field loss, or eye pain. He wears progressive bifocal lenses for distance and near vision and reports these are adequate. He does not drive at night due to difficulty with glare.

His most recent A1c was 7.4% (June 2024). Blood pressure has been well-controlled per his cardiologist. He has CKD stage 3b which can accelerate diabetic retinopathy.

diagnoses: Early nuclear sclerotic cataracts bilateral (noted 2022) — not visually significant
ocularHistory.lastExam: December 2023 — bilateral mild NPDR, no DME, stable

sections.currentMedications: See comprehensive medication list in chart. No ophthalmic medications. Not on hydroxychloroquine or other medications with ocular toxicity risk.

visualAcuity.note: Corrected acuity stable from prior visit. Right eye slightly worse than left — consistent with slightly more advanced cataract on right.

intraocularPressure.interpretation: Normal (target <21 mmHg). No concern for glaucoma.

rightEye.anteriorChamber: Deep, quiet, no cells or flare
rightEye.lens: Grade 2+ nuclear sclerosis (mild progression from grade 1-2+ last year)

rightEye.opticDisc: Pink, sharp margins, cup-to-disc ratio 0.3, no pallor, no neovascularization of the disc (NVD)
rightEye.macula: Flat, no edema, no hard exudates, no hemorrhage within 1 disc diameter of fovea
rightEye.vessels: Mild arteriolar narrowing and AV nicking consistent with hypertensive changes. No venous beading or looping.
rightEye.periphery: Scattered microaneurysms in nasal and temporal retina (approximately 15-20 total). Three small dot-blot hemorrhages in the temporal mid-periphery. No cotton-wool spots. No intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA). No neovascularization elsewhere (NVE). No vitreous hemorrhage.
rightEye.classification: Mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy (ETDRS severity level 35)

leftEye.opticDisc: Pink, sharp margins, cup-to-disc ratio 0.3, no NVD
leftEye.vessels: Mild arteriolar narrowing. No venous beading.
leftEye.periphery: Scattered microaneurysms (approximately 10-12 total). Two small dot-blot hemorrhages. No cotton-wool spots, no IRMA, no NVE, no vitreous hemorrhage.
leftEye.classification: Mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy (ETDRS severity level 35)

sections.assessment:
72-year-old male with type 2 diabetes mellitus x 15 years with bilateral mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy, stable from prior examination. No diabetic macular edema on clinical exam or OCT. Early nuclear sclerotic cataracts bilateral, right greater than left, not visually significant at this time.

Risk factors for progression:
- Duration of diabetes (15 years)
- CKD stage 3b (accelerates retinopathy)
- A1c 7.4% (adequate but not tight)
- Hypertension (well-controlled)

Protective factors:
- A1c at reasonable target for his age and comorbidities (<8%)
- Blood pressure well-controlled
- On SGLT2 inhibitor (emerging evidence of retinal benefit)
- No macular edema
- Stable over past 3 years of observation

items: Continue glycemic control — A1c goal <8% per endocrinology, current 7.4% is appropriate
Blood pressure control remains critical for retinopathy progression prevention — current management adequate
No ophthalmic treatment needed at this time — mild NPDR does not require laser or intravitreal injections
Return in 6 months (June 2025) for repeat dilated examination given CKD as progression risk factor
Standard interval for mild NPDR is 6-12 months — choosing 6-month interval due to CKD
Will repeat OCT macula at next visit
If any sudden visual changes (new floaters, flashing lights, blurry vision, dark spot in vision), patient should be seen urgently — do not wait for scheduled appointment
Early nuclear sclerotic cataracts bilateral — no surgery indicated at this time
Corrected acuity 20/30 OD and 20/25 OS is functional
Night driving difficulty likely related to cataracts — patient has appropriately self-limited night driving
Will monitor progression — surgery discussion when acuity drops below 20/40 or patient reports significant functional limitation

sections.patientEducation: Discussed findings with Mr. Morrison. Explained that his diabetic eye disease remains mild and stable, which is very good news. Emphasized that the best things he can do for his eyes are to continue controlling his blood sugar and blood pressure. Explained warning signs that should prompt immediate evaluation: sudden increase in floaters, flashing lights, a curtain or shadow in his vision, or sudden blurry vision in either eye. Patient verbalized understanding. Reviewed that he is due back in 6 months rather than 12 months because his kidney disease puts him at slightly higher risk for progression.
sections.plan:
1. Bilateral mild NPDR — stable, no treatment needed
2. No diabetic macular edema
3. Continue systemic management per PCP, endocrinology, and nephrology (A1c <8%, BP <130/80)
4. Return in 6 months (June 2025) for dilated exam and OCT
5. Urgent evaluation if sudden visual symptoms
6. Updated spectacle prescription provided — minor change in right eye

attestation:
I personally examined this patient, performed dilated funduscopy, reviewed OCT imaging, and formulated the assessment and plan.

Electronically signed by: Karen Liu, MD, FACS
Date/Time: 12/10/2024 4:30 PM
